title: Rumpelstilzchen
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 55. Text from Wikisource („Rumpelstilzchen (1857)“, twice proofread against the original). Fresh translation made directly from the German; 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The fifty-fifth tale (KHM 55). A miller’s boast that his daughter can spin straw into gold traps her before a greedy king, until a strange little man spins for her at the price of her first child — redeemable only if she can guess his name. 1857 spelling is preserved (ward, gieng, Thüre, Wuth).

sentences.t: Es war einmal ein Müller, der war arm, aber er hatte eine schöne Tochter.
sentences.l: There was once a miller, who was poor, but he had a beautiful daughter.
sentences.i: Once upon a time there was a miller who was poor, but he had a beautiful daughter.
sentences.n: „Es war einmal“ is the fixed fairy-tale opening, ‘once upon a time.’ „der war arm“ resumes ein Müller with a demonstrative der (‘he/that one’) — German constantly points back to a noun this way instead of with a relative pronoun.

sentences.t: Nun traf es sich, daß er mit dem König zu sprechen kam, und um sich ein Ansehen zu geben, sagte er zu ihm „ich habe eine Tochter, die kann Stroh zu Gold spinnen.“
sentences.l: Now it happened that he came to speak with the king, and in order to give himself importance, he said to him “I have a daughter, she can spin straw into gold.”
sentences.i: Now it came about that he had occasion to speak with the king, and to give himself an air of importance he said to him: “I have a daughter who can spin straw into gold.”
sentences.n: „es traf sich“ = it happened, it came about (sich treffen, impersonal). „sich ein Ansehen geben“ = to give oneself standing/importance. „die kann“ again resumes Tochter with a demonstrative.

sentences.t: Der König sprach zum Müller „das ist eine Kunst, die mir wohl gefällt, wenn deine Tochter so geschickt ist, wie du sagst, so bring sie Morgen in mein Schloß, da will ich sie auf die Probe stellen.“
sentences.l: The king spoke to the miller “that is an art that pleases me well; if your daughter is so skillful as you say, then bring her tomorrow into my castle, there I will put her to the test.”
sentences.i: The king said to the miller: “That is an art that pleases me well; if your daughter is as skillful as you say, then bring her to my castle tomorrow, and I will put her to the test.”
sentences.n: „auf die Probe stellen“ = to put to the test. Note that „Morgen“ here (capitalized in the 1857 text) is the adverb ‘tomorrow,’ not the noun ‘morning.’

sentences.t: Als nun das Mädchen zu ihm gebracht ward, führte er es in eine Kammer, die ganz voll Stroh lag, gab ihr Rad und Haspel und sprach „jetzt mache dich an die Arbeit, und wenn du diese Nacht durch bis morgen früh dieses Stroh nicht zu Gold versponnen hast, so mußt du sterben.“
sentences.l: When now the girl was brought to him, he led her into a chamber that lay quite full of straw, gave her wheel and reel and spoke “now set yourself to the work, and if by early tomorrow you have not spun this straw into gold through this night, then you must die.”
sentences.i: When the girl was brought to him, he led her into a chamber that lay quite full of straw, gave her a spinning-wheel and reel and said: “Now set to work, and if by early tomorrow you have not spun this straw into gold through the night, then you must die.”
sentences.n: „ward“ = archaic past of ‘became/was’ (modern wurde), here forming the passive. „mach dich an die Arbeit“ = set to work. Rad = the spinning-wheel, Haspel = the reel for winding off the yarn.

sentences.t: Darauf schloß er die Kammer selbst zu, und sie blieb allein darin.
sentences.l: Thereupon he locked the chamber up himself, and she remained alone therein.
sentences.i: Then he locked the chamber himself, and she was left alone inside.
sentences.n: „zuschließen“ = to lock up (separable: schloß … zu). Don’t confuse this verb schloß with the noun Schloß ‘castle’ above — identical spelling, different words.

sentences.t: Da saß nun die arme Müllerstochter und wußte um ihr Leben keinen Rath: sie verstand gar nichts davon, wie man Stroh zu Gold spinnen konnte, und ihre Angst ward immer größer, daß sie endlich zu weinen anfieng.
sentences.l: There now sat the poor miller’s daughter and knew, for her life, no counsel: she understood nothing at all of how one could spin straw into gold, and her fear grew ever greater, so that she at last began to weep.
sentences.i: So there sat the poor miller’s daughter, and for the life of her could find no way out: she understood nothing at all of how straw might be spun into gold, and her fear grew greater and greater, until at last she began to weep.
sentences.n: „keinen Rath wissen“ (1857 Rath = modern Rat) = to be at a loss, know no way out. „ward immer größer“ = grew ever greater. „anfieng“ (modern anfing) = began, from separable anfangen.

sentences.t: Da gieng auf einmal die Thüre auf, und trat ein kleines Männchen herein und sprach „guten Abend, Jungfer Müllerin, warum weint sie so sehr?“
sentences.l: Then all at once the door went open, and a little manikin stepped in and spoke “good evening, Miss Miller, why does she weep so sorely?”
sentences.i: Then all at once the door opened, and a little man stepped in and said: “Good evening, Mistress Miller, why do you weep so bitterly?”
sentences.n: „gieng … auf“ = opened (aufgehen, separable; 1857 gieng for ging). Note the little man addresses the girl in the third person, „weint sie“ — an old deferential form of ‘you.’ Männchen (and Männlein, used later) are diminutives of Mann.

sentences.t: „Ach,“ antwortete das Mädchen, „ich soll Stroh zu Gold spinnen, und verstehe das nicht.“
sentences.l: “Ah,” answered the girl, “I am to spin straw into gold, and do not understand it.”
sentences.i: “Oh,” answered the girl, “I am to spin straw into gold, and I don’t know how.”

sentences.t: Sprach das Männchen „was gibst du mir, wenn ich dirs spinne?“
sentences.l: Spoke the manikin “what do you give me, if I spin it for you?”
sentences.i: Said the little man: “What will you give me if I spin it for you?”
sentences.n: „dirs“ = dir es (‘it for you’), contracted. German fronts the verb here, „Sprach das Männchen,“ ‘said the little man.’

sentences.t: „Mein Halsband“ sagte das Mädchen.
sentences.l: “My necklace” said the girl.

sentences.t: Das Männchen nahm das Halsband, setzte sich vor das Rädchen, und schnurr, schnurr, schnurr, dreimal gezogen, war die Spule voll.
sentences.l: The manikin took the necklace, set itself before the little wheel, and whirr, whirr, whirr, three times drawn, the spool was full.
sentences.i: The little man took the necklace, sat down before the little wheel, and whirr, whirr, whirr — three pulls, and the spool was full.
sentences.n: „schnurr“ = the whirring sound of the spinning-wheel. „dreimal gezogen“ = ‘three times drawn/pulled’ — a terse participial phrase, ‘after three pulls.’

sentences.t: Dann steckte es eine andere auf, und schnurr, schnurr, schnurr, dreimal gezogen, war auch die zweite voll: und so giengs fort bis zum Morgen, da war alles Stroh versponnen, und alle Spulen waren voll Gold.
sentences.l: Then it put another one on, and whirr, whirr, whirr, three times drawn, the second too was full: and so it went on until the morning, when all the straw was spun up, and all the spools were full of gold.
sentences.i: Then he put on another, and whirr, whirr, whirr — three pulls, and the second too was full; and so it went on till morning, when all the straw was spun and every spool was full of gold.
sentences.n: „giengs fort“ = gieng es fort, ‘it went on’ (fortgehen). „versponnen“ = spun up, spun to the last (past participle of verspinnen).

sentences.t: Bei Sonnenaufgang kam schon der König und als er das Gold erblickte, erstaunte er und freute sich, aber sein Herz ward nur noch goldgieriger.
sentences.l: At sunrise the king already came, and when he caught sight of the gold, he was astonished and rejoiced, but his heart became only the more gold-greedy.
sentences.i: At sunrise the king came already, and when he caught sight of the gold he was amazed and delighted, but his heart only grew the greedier for gold.
sentences.n: „goldgieriger“ = ‘greedier for gold’ (comparative of goldgierig; 1857 spells the ending -gierig).

sentences.t: Er ließ die Müllerstochter in eine andere Kammer voll Stroh bringen, die noch viel größer war, und befahl ihr das auch in einer Nacht zu spinnen, wenn ihr das Leben lieb wäre.
sentences.l: He had the miller’s daughter brought into another chamber full of straw, which was still much larger, and commanded her to spin that too in one night, if her life were dear to her.
sentences.i: He had the miller’s daughter brought to another chamber full of straw, far larger still, and ordered her to spin that too in a single night, if she valued her life.
sentences.n: „ließ … bringen“ = had (someone) brought — lassen + infinitive, the causative. „wenn ihr das Leben lieb wäre“ = ‘if life were dear to her,’ subjunctive of the veiled threat.

sentences.t: Das Mädchen wußte sich nicht zu helfen und weinte, da gieng abermals die Thüre auf, und das kleine Männchen erschien und sprach „was gibst du mir, wenn ich dir das Stroh zu Gold spinne?“
sentences.l: The girl knew not how to help herself and wept; then once again the door went open, and the little manikin appeared and spoke “what do you give me, if I spin the straw into gold for you?”
sentences.i: The girl did not know what to do and wept; then once again the door opened, and the little man appeared and said: “What will you give me if I spin the straw into gold for you?”
sentences.n: „sich nicht zu helfen wissen“ = to be helpless, not to know what to do. abermals = once again (archaic).

sentences.t: „Meinen Ring von dem Finger“ antwortete das Mädchen.
sentences.l: “My ring from my finger” answered the girl.

sentences.t: Das Männchen nahm den Ring, fieng wieder an zu schnurren mit dem Rade und hatte bis zum Morgen alles Stroh zu glänzendem Gold gesponnen.
sentences.l: The manikin took the ring, began again to whirr with the wheel, and had by morning spun all the straw into gleaming gold.
sentences.i: The little man took the ring, set the wheel whirring again, and by morning had spun all the straw into gleaming gold.
sentences.n: „fieng … an“ = began (anfangen, separable; 1857 fieng for fing). „mit dem Rade“ — Rade is the old dative of Rad, the spinning-wheel.

sentences.t: Der König freute sich über die Maßen bei dem Anblick, war aber noch immer nicht Goldes satt, sondern ließ die Müllerstochter in eine noch größere Kammer voll Stroh bringen und sprach „die mußt du noch in dieser Nacht verspinnen: gelingt dirs aber, so sollst du meine Gemahlin werden.“
sentences.l: The king rejoiced beyond measure at the sight, but was still not sated with gold, and instead had the miller’s daughter brought into a yet larger chamber full of straw and spoke “this one you must spin up this very night: but if you succeed at it, then you shall become my wife.”
sentences.i: The king was overjoyed beyond measure at the sight, but was still not sated with gold, so he had the miller’s daughter brought to an even larger chamber full of straw, and said: “This you must spin up in this very night; but if you succeed, you shall become my wife.”
sentences.n: „über die Maßen“ = beyond measure, exceedingly. „Goldes satt“ = sated with gold (genitive object). „gelingt dirs“ = gelingt es dir, ‘if you succeed at it’ — gelingen is impersonal and takes a dative.

sentences.t: „Wenns auch eine Müllerstochter ist,“ dachte er, „eine reichere Frau finde ich in der ganzen Welt nicht.“
sentences.l: “Even if she is a miller’s daughter,” he thought, “a richer wife I find in the whole world not.”
sentences.i: “Even if she is only a miller’s daughter,” he thought, “I shall not find a richer wife in all the world.”
sentences.n: „Wenns“ = wenn es, contracted. Note the fronted object: „eine reichere Frau finde ich … nicht.“

sentences.t: Als das Mädchen allein war, kam das Männlein zum drittenmal wieder und sprach „was gibst du mir, wenn ich dir noch diesmal das Stroh spinne?“
sentences.l: When the girl was alone, the manikin came again for the third time and spoke “what do you give me, if I spin the straw for you this time too?”
sentences.i: When the girl was alone, the little man came again a third time and said: “What will you give me if I spin the straw for you this time as well?”

sentences.t: „Ich habe nichts mehr, das ich geben könnte“ antwortete das Mädchen.
sentences.l: “I have nothing more that I could give” answered the girl.

sentences.t: „So versprich mir, wenn du Königin wirst, dein erstes Kind.“
sentences.l: “Then promise me, when you become queen, your first child.”
sentences.i: “Then promise me your first child, when you become queen.”
sentences.n: „versprich“ = imperative of versprechen, ‘promise.’

sentences.t: „Wer weiß wie das noch geht“ dachte die Müllerstochter und wußte sich auch in der Noth nicht anders zu helfen; sie versprach also dem Männchen was es verlangte, und das Männchen spann dafür noch einmal das Stroh zu Gold.
sentences.l: “Who knows how that will yet turn out,” thought the miller’s daughter, and in her distress knew no other way to help herself either; so she promised the manikin what it demanded, and the manikin spun the straw into gold for that once more.
sentences.i: “Who knows how things may yet turn out,” thought the miller’s daughter, and in her distress she could see no other way; so she promised the little man what he asked, and in return he once more spun the straw into gold.
sentences.n: „wie das noch geht“ = how it will yet turn out. Noth (1857) = modern Not, ‘distress, need.’ „spann“ = past of spinnen.

sentences.t: Und als am Morgen der König kam und alles fand wie er gewünscht hatte, so hielt er Hochzeit mit ihr, und die schöne Müllerstochter ward eine Königin.
sentences.l: And when in the morning the king came and found everything as he had wished, then he held wedding with her, and the beautiful miller’s daughter became a queen.
sentences.i: And when the king came in the morning and found everything as he had wished, he held his wedding with her, and the beautiful miller’s daughter became a queen.

sentences.t: Über ein Jahr brachte sie ein schönes Kind zur Welt und dachte gar nicht mehr an das Männchen: da trat es plötzlich in ihre Kammer und sprach „nun gib mir was du versprochen hast.“
sentences.l: After a year she brought a beautiful child into the world and thought no more at all of the manikin: then it suddenly stepped into her chamber and spoke “now give me what you have promised.”
sentences.i: After a year she brought a beautiful child into the world, and thought no more at all of the little man — when suddenly he stepped into her chamber and said: “Now give me what you promised.”
sentences.n: „über ein Jahr“ = after a year (here über = ‘in the course of, after’). „ein Kind zur Welt bringen“ = to give birth (lit. ‘bring into the world’). gib = imperative of geben.

sentences.t: Die Königin erschrack und bot dem Männchen alle Reichthümer des Königreichs an, wenn es ihr das Kind lassen wollte: aber das Männchen sprach „nein, etwas lebendes ist mir lieber als alle Schätze der Welt.“
sentences.l: The queen took fright and offered the manikin all the riches of the kingdom, if it would leave her the child: but the manikin spoke “no, something living is dearer to me than all the treasures of the world.”
sentences.i: The queen was terrified and offered the little man all the riches of the kingdom if he would leave her the child; but the little man said: “No — something living is dearer to me than all the treasures of the world.”
sentences.n: erschrack (1857) = modern erschrak, ‘took fright.’ „bot … an“ = offered (anbieten, separable). „etwas lebendes“ = something living.

sentences.t: Da fieng die Königin so an zu jammern und zu weinen, daß das Männchen Mitleiden mit ihr hatte: „drei Tage will ich dir Zeit lassen,“ sprach er, „wenn du bis dahin meinen Namen weißt, so sollst du dein Kind behalten.“
sentences.l: Then the queen began so to wail and to weep that the manikin had compassion with her: “three days will I leave you time,” he spoke, “if by then you know my name, then you shall keep your child.”
sentences.i: Then the queen began to wail and weep so hard that the little man took pity on her: “Three days I will give you,” he said; “if by then you know my name, you shall keep your child.”
sentences.n: „fieng … an“ = began (anfangen). Mitleiden (older form of Mitleid) = pity, compassion. behalten = to keep.

sentences.t: Nun besann sich die Königin die ganze Nacht über auf alle Namen, die sie jemals gehört hatte, und schickte einen Boten über Land, der sollte sich erkundigen weit und breit was es sonst noch für Namen gäbe.
sentences.l: Now the queen bethought herself the whole night through of all the names that she had ever heard, and sent a messenger over the land, who was to inquire far and wide what other names there might be.
sentences.i: Now the queen pondered all night long over every name she had ever heard, and sent a messenger across the land to inquire far and wide what other names there might be.
sentences.n: „sich besinnen auf“ = to call to mind, ponder. „die ganze Nacht über“ = all night long. „was es für Namen gäbe“ = what names there might be — gäbe is subjunctive II of the reported inquiry; „was für“ = ‘what kind of.’

sentences.t: Als am andern Tag das Männchen kam, fieng sie an mit Caspar, Melchior, Balzer, und sagte alle Namen, die sie wußte, nach der Reihe her, aber bei jedem sprach das Männlein „so heiß ich nicht.“
sentences.l: When on the next day the manikin came, she began with Caspar, Melchior, Balzer, and said all the names that she knew one after another, but at each one the manikin spoke “so I am not called.”
sentences.i: When the little man came the next day, she began with Caspar, Melchior, Balthasar, and reeled off all the names she knew one after another, but at each one the little man said: “That is not my name.”
sentences.n: Caspar, Melchior and Balzer (Balthasar) are the traditional names of the Three Kings. „nach der Reihe hersagen“ = to recite in order, one after another. „so heiß ich nicht“ = that is not what I am called.

sentences.t: Den zweiten Tag ließ sie in der Nachbarschaft herumfragen wie die Leute da genannt würden, und sagte dem Männlein die ungewöhnlichsten und seltsamsten Namen vor, „heißt du vielleicht Rippenbiest oder Hammelswade oder Schnürbein?“ aber es antwortete immer „so heiß ich nicht.“
sentences.l: On the second day she had inquiries made around the neighborhood as to what the people there were called, and recited to the manikin the most unusual and strangest names, “are you perhaps called Rib-beast or Mutton-shank or Lace-leg?” but it always answered “so I am not called.”
sentences.i: On the second day she had people ask around the neighborhood what the folk there were called, and reeled off to the little man the most unusual and outlandish names: “Is your name perhaps Rib-beast, or Mutton-shank, or Lace-leg?” — but he always answered: “That is not my name.”
sentences.n: „ließ … herumfragen“ = had (people) ask around (causative lassen). Rippenbiest, Hammelswade and Schnürbein are comic invented names — ‘rib-beast,’ ‘mutton-shank,’ ‘lace-leg.’ „vorsagen“ = to recite, say out to someone.

sentences.t: Den dritten Tag kam der Bote wieder zurück und erzählte „neue Namen habe ich keinen einzigen finden können, aber wie ich an einen hohen Berg um die Waldecke kam, wo Fuchs und Has sich gute Nacht sagen, so sah ich da ein kleines Haus, und vor dem Haus brannte ein Feuer, und um das Feuer sprang ein gar zu lächerliches Männchen, hüpfte auf einem Bein und schrie
sentences.l: On the third day the messenger came back again and related “new names I could not find a single one, but as I came around the corner of the forest to a high mountain, where fox and hare say good night to each other, there I saw a little house, and before the house a fire was burning, and around the fire a truly all-too-ridiculous manikin was leaping, hopped on one leg and cried
sentences.i: On the third day the messenger came back and told her: “New names I could not find a single one; but as I came to a high mountain, round the corner of the forest, where fox and hare bid each other good night, I saw a little house there, and before the house a fire was burning, and round the fire a most ridiculous little man was leaping — he hopped on one leg and cried:
sentences.n: „wo Fuchs und Has sich gute Nacht sagen“ = a set phrase for the middle of nowhere, the back of beyond (Has = old short form of Hase, hare). „keinen einzigen“ = not a single one. „gar zu“ = far too, all too. The sentence runs straight on into the rhyme, so it has no closing quotation mark.

sentences.t:
„heute back ich, morgen brau ich,
übermorgen hol ich der Königin ihr Kind;
ach, wie gut ist daß niemand weiß
daß ich Rumpelstilzchen heiß!“
sentences.l:
“today I bake, tomorrow I brew,
the day after tomorrow I fetch the queen’s child;
ah, how good it is that no one knows
that I am called Rumpelstilzchen!”
sentences.i: “Today I bake, tomorrow I brew, / the day after I fetch the queen’s child; / oh, how good that nobody knows / that Rumpelstiltskin is my name!”
sentences.n: The name-rhyme, the heart of the tale. „back“ and „brau“ are 1st sg. of backen and brauen with the -e dropped. „der Königin ihr Kind“ = ‘the queen her child,’ a colloquial dative-possessive (= the queen’s child). „heiß“ = 1st sg. of heißen, ‘I am called,’ the -e again dropped to rhyme with weiß. The name Rumpelstilzchen packs rumpeln (‘to rumble, make a racket’) + Stilz/Stelze (‘stilt’) + the diminutive -chen; it is kept untranslated.

sentences.t: Da könnt ihr denken wie die Königin froh war, als sie den Namen hörte, und als bald hernach das Männlein herein trat und fragte „nun, Frau Königin, wie heiß ich?“ fragte sie erst „heißest du Kunz?“ „Nein.“ „Heißest du Heinz?“ „Nein.“
sentences.l: There you can imagine how glad the queen was when she heard the name, and when soon thereafter the manikin stepped in and asked “now, Lady Queen, what am I called?” she first asked “are you called Kunz?” “No.” “Are you called Heinz?” “No.”
sentences.i: Now you can imagine how glad the queen was when she heard the name; and when, soon afterward, the little man stepped in and asked, “Well, my lady queen, what is my name?” — she asked first, “Is your name Kunz?” “No.” “Is your name Heinz?” “No.”
sentences.n: „Da könnt ihr denken“ = the narrator turning to the listeners, ‘you can just imagine.’ hernach = afterward (archaic). Kunz and Heinz are homely everyday names (short for Konrad and Heinrich), set out deliberately before the real one.

sentences.t: „Heißt du etwa Rumpelstilzchen?“
sentences.l: “Is your name perhaps Rumpelstilzchen?”
sentences.n: „etwa“ here = ‘perhaps, by any chance’ (not ‘approximately’) — the feigned casualness of the winning guess.

sentences.t: „Das hat dir der Teufel gesagt, das hat dir der Teufel gesagt“ schrie das Männlein und stieß mit dem rechten Fuß vor Zorn so tief in die Erde, daß es bis an den Leib hineinfuhr, dann packte es in seiner Wuth den linken Fuß mit beiden Händen und riß sich selbst mitten entzwei.
sentences.l: “The devil has told you that, the devil has told you that” cried the manikin, and thrust with its right foot in anger so deep into the earth that it drove in up to the body; then it seized in its fury the left foot with both hands and tore itself right in two.
sentences.i: “The devil told you that! The devil told you that!” shrieked the little man, and in his anger drove his right foot so deep into the ground that he sank in up to his waist; then in his fury he seized his left foot with both hands and tore himself clean in two.
sentences.n: „vor Zorn“ = out of anger. „bis an den Leib hineinfuhr“ = drove/plunged in up to the body (hineinfahren). Wuth (1857) = modern Wut, ‘fury.’ „entzwei reißen“ = to tear in two — the grisly ending, left unsoftened.
